meaning: to become sunny, clear up, be sunny
lesson:
晴はれる is the sky clearing. Clouds part, sun comes through, the world brightens. 明日あしたは晴はれるかな (will it clear up tomorrow, I wonder). It's intransitive — nobody makes the sky clear; it clears on its own. The noun form 晴はれ is what you'll see on weather forecasts for sunny weather.

But 晴はれる isn't limited to meteorology. 気持きもちが晴はれる (my feelings cleared up — I feel better). 疑うたがいが晴はれる (suspicion is cleared). The metaphor is universal: clarity after obscurity, relief after burden. When something that was weighing on you lifts, it 晴はれる.

The kanji 晴せい is 日にち (sun) + 青あお (blue). Sun plus blue — blue skies. It's one of the most visually poetic kanji, describing the exact thing you see when the weather is fine. The compound 晴天せいてん (fine weather) doubles down on the image.

Particles: 晴はれる takes が — the sky or weather is the subject that clears. 空そらが晴はれる (the sky clears). Often used without an explicit subject when the context is obviously weather.

Passive/Causative: Almost never used — you can't be passively cleared-up or make something sunny. The verb's nature is purely spontaneous.

一段 Ichidan自動詞
Dictionaryはれるが
Negativeはれないが
Politeはれますが
Polite negはれませんが
Pastはれたが
Polite pastはれましたが
て-formはれてが
Volitionalはれようが
Polite volはれましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—

Cond ～たらはれたらが

きたない covers dirty in every sense. A muddy floor is 汚きたない. Messy handwriting is 汚きたない. Cheating at a game is 汚きたない. A corrupt politician plays 汚きたない tricks. The word doesn't separate physical filth from moral filth — both are contamination, both violate a standard of purity.

The kanji 汚お has the water radical 氵さんずい plus 亏う — muddy water, water that's been spoiled. If you've ever seen a clear stream turn brown after rain, that's the image living inside this character.

Sound anchor: kita-nai — say it and feel the slight recoil in the sound. The き is sharp, the たない trails off in disgust. Japanese kids yell きたない！ the way English kids yell "Eww!" — the word itself functions as an exclamation of disgust, not just a descriptor.

Dictionaryきたない
Negativeきたなくない
Pastきたなかった
Past negきたなくなかった
Adverbきたなく
て-formきたなくて

茶ちゃ (tea) + 色いろ (color) = tea-color = brown. The color of brewed green tea — not the bright green of matcha powder, but the amber-brown liquid that comes from steeping leaves in hot water. Japanese green tea, when brewed, is actually quite brown. This makes perfect sense once you've seen it; the word is describing what's in your teacup.

Like 黄色きいろ, this is a noun-color that can optionally take い to become an adjective: 茶色ちゃいろい犬いぬ or 茶色ちゃいろの犬いぬ — both mean "brown dog." The adjective form 茶色ちゃいろい is slightly more casual and spoken; 茶色ちゃいろの is slightly more neutral. Either works.

Many Japanese color names are this transparent: 桃色ももいろ (peach-color = pink), 灰色はいいろ (ash-color = gray), 水色みずいろ (water-color = light blue). Once you see the pattern, color vocabulary gets easy — it's just [thing] + 色.

meaning: to become empty, become less crowded (no direct object)
lesson:
The verb behind お腹なかが空すいた — the sentence you'll say every day. 空すく means "to become empty" or "to thin out," and it applies to anything with contents that deplete: stomachs, trains, parking lots, schedules. 電車でんしゃが空すいている (the train is uncrowded — it has emptied out). 席せきが空すいている (a seat is available — it's empty).

The kanji 空くう means sky or empty — the same character used in 空港くうこう (airport) and 空気くうき (air/atmosphere). The sky is the ultimate empty space. A cave (穴あな, the radical on top) with work (工こう) below — a carved-out hollow. Emptiness made by design.

Watch the reading: 空すく can be read すく (to become empty) or あく (to open up, become available). お腹なかが空すく is always すく. 席せきが空すく can be either, depending on the speaker, but あく is more common for physical spaces opening up.

Particles: 空すく takes が — the thing that empties is the subject. お腹なかが空すいた. 道みちが空すいている (the road is clear).

Passive/Causative: Essentially unused — emptying is a state change that happens to things, not something you do to or suffer from people.

五段 Godan自動詞

Negativeすかないが
Politeすきますが
Polite negすきませんが

Polite pastすきましたが
て-formすいてが
Volitionalすこうが
Polite volすきましょうが

Causative—

meaning: to go up, ascend, rise (no direct object)
lesson:
Three kanji can be read のぼる, each capturing a different flavor of ascent. 上のぼる is the general-purpose one — going up stairs, climbing a slope, the sun rising. 登のぼる is specifically climbing with effort — mountains, rock walls, ladders. 昇のぼる is more abstract or celestial — the sun, promotions, things ascending in status. In practice, 上のぼる covers most situations.

階段かいだんを上のぼる (go up the stairs). 坂さかを上のぼる (go up the hill). Notice the を — this isn't marking a direct object (the verb is intransitive). It's the movement-を that marks the path traversed, the same one you see in 道みちを歩あるく (walk along the road) and 空そらを飛とぶ (fly through the sky).

The pair verb is 上あげる — to raise something. 上のぼる: the thing goes up on its own. 上あげる: you lift it up. Prices 上あがる (another intransitive form). You 上あげる your hand.

Particles: 上のぼる takes を (the path/surface you ascend) or に (the destination you ascend to). 山やまに上のぼる (go up to the mountain).

Passive に上のぼられる: rare but possible — 先さきに上のぼられた (they went up before me — I was left behind).

Causative を／に上のぼらせる: making someone climb. 子供こどもに階段かいだんを上のぼらせる (make the kid take the stairs).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 上げる (あげる)

Polite negのぼりませんを
Pastのぼったを

て-formのぼってを
Volitionalのぼろうを

Causativeのぼらせるを／に
Caus-passのぼらせられるに

Imperativeのぼれを
Imp なさいのぼりなさいを

Cond ～たらのぼったらを

ご飯はん means two things, and both of them are essential. First: cooked rice. The white stuff in the bowl, steamed, sticky, the foundation of Japanese cuisine for thousands of years. Second: a meal — any meal. ご飯はん食たべた？ doesn't ask if you ate rice specifically. It asks if you've eaten. Rice and eating are so intertwined in Japanese culture that the word for one became the word for the other.

The ご is an honorific prefix — the same 御ご that appears in ご主人しゅじん, ご飯はん, and お茶ちゃ (which uses the other honorific, お). Rice is important enough to get permanent honorific treatment. The bare form 飯めし exists but sounds rough and masculine — 飯めし食くおうぜ (let's eat) is how guys talk to their buddies, not how you address your mother-in-law.

Japanese distinguishes stages of rice: 米こめ (uncooked grain), ご飯はん (cooked rice), 稲いね (rice plant in the field). English uses "rice" for all three. The fact that Japanese has three separate words tells you everything about how central this grain is to the culture.

日にち as a counter is irregular in ways that will test your patience. The first ten days of the month have readings that follow no discernible pattern — they're inherited from an ancient native Japanese counting system that predates Chinese influence. 一日ついたち, 二日ふつか, 三日みっか... each one is its own word that you simply memorize. After the tenth, it switches to the more predictable Chinese system: 十一日じゅういちにち, 十二日じゅうににち. With one exception: 二十日はつか. Just because.

When 日にち means "day" as a generic counter (not a date), it uses にち or か depending on the number: 三日間みっかかん (three days), 十五日間じゅうごにちかん (fifteen days).

The kanji 日にち is a sun — a circle with a line through it, compressed into a rectangle over millennia of calligraphic efficiency. It's one of the first kanji anyone learns and one of the most common in the language: 日本にほん (Japan — "sun origin"), 毎日まいにち (every day), 日曜日にちようび (Sunday). The sun marks the day. It always has.

髪かみ (hair on the head) + の + 毛け (fur/hair/strand). The の connects them possessively: the individual strands that belong to your head-hair. 髪かみ by itself means hair as a collective mass — your hairstyle, your hair overall. 髪かみの毛け zooms in to individual strands. 髪かみの毛けが一本いっぽん落おちている (there's a single hair on the ground). スープに髪かみの毛けが入はいっていた (there was a hair in my soup).

The kanji 髪かみ is dense — 髟かみがしら (the long-hair radical, which you'll recognize on top of 髭ひげ (mustache) too) over 友とも (friend) and other components. It's one of the more complex everyday kanji, which seems fitting for something as unruly as hair.

毛け on its own is broader than head hair — it covers any body hair, animal fur, wool, even peach fuzz. 猫ねこの毛け (cat fur). 毛布もうふ (blanket — literally "fur cloth"). The character shows strands growing from a surface.

meaning: to turn on, switch on (direct object)
lesson:
The partner to 消けす (to turn off). つける flips the switch on: 電気でんきをつける (turn on the light). テレビをつける (turn on the TV). エアコンをつける (turn on the AC). Its intransitive partner is つく — the light comes on by itself.

But つける is written with different kanji depending on meaning, and there are several. 付つける means to attach, stick on, apply. 着つける means to put on (clothing, especially to the body). 漬つける means to pickle or soak. When it means "turn on," it's usually written in hiragana — つける — because the "switch on" meaning doesn't map cleanly to any single kanji.

The reason all these meanings share a sound is that the core concept is "to put something into contact with something else." Light to a room, a sticker to a surface, a body to clothes, vegetables to brine. Attachment, application, activation — they're all つける at root.

Particles: つける takes を — the thing you switch on is the direct object. 電気でんきをつける.

Potential がつけられる: が — what can be turned on becomes the subject.

Passive につけられる: に marks who turned it on. 夜中よなかにテレビをつけられた (someone turned on the TV in the middle of the night — and it woke me up).

Causative に…をつけさせる: に marks who you make turn it on.

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: つく (intransitive)
Dictionaryつけるを
Negativeつけないを
Politeつけますを
Polite negつけませんを
Pastつけたを
Polite pastつけましたを
て-formつけてを
Volitionalつけようを
Polite volつけましょうを
Passiveつけられるに
Causativeつけさせるに／を
Caus-passつけさせられるに
Potentialつけられるが
Imperativeつけろを
Imp なさいつけなさいを
Cond ～ばつければを
Cond ～たらつけたらを

月つき (moon) + 曜日ようび = Moon-day. English Monday is also Moon-day, from Old English Mōnandæg. Two languages, no shared ancestry, same answer — because both inherited the same Babylonian system that assigned celestial bodies to weekdays.

Here's what's worth noticing: 月つき does triple duty in Japanese, and the three jobs look identical on the page. 月つき the moon. 月つき the month (because months track the moon's cycle — 一月いちがつ, いちがつ, January). And 月つき the meat radical, hiding in body-part kanji like 腕うで, 腹はら, 胸むね — where it doesn't mean "moon" at all, but derives from a completely different character (肉にく) that got squished into the same shape. Three origins, one visual form. When you see 月つき on the left side of a kanji, it's probably meat. Everywhere else, it's the moon.

月曜日げつようび shortens to 月曜げつよう in casual speech and on calendars. All seven days allow this — drop the 日にち and the meaning stays the same.

meaning: to enter, go inside (no direct object)
lesson:
The reading trips people up. 入にゅう looks like it should be a simple kanji with a simple reading — and it is, but it has two common verb forms with different readings: 入はいる (to enter) and 入いれる (to put in). The intransitive/transitive pair. You はいる the room. You いれる coffee into the cup.

部屋へやに入はいる (enter the room). お風呂ふろに入はいる (take a bath — literally "enter the bath"). 大学だいがくに入はいる (enter university — enroll). The word works for physical entry, institutional enrollment, and even joining groups: チームに入はいる (join a team).

The kanji 入にゅう is one of the simplest — two strokes that look like an arrow pointing inward, or a person ducking through a doorway. It's also the radical found in 込こむ (to be crowded, go into) and related characters. Entry, inward motion — the visual matches the meaning.

Particles: 入はいる takes に — the place you enter. 店みせに入はいる (enter the store). You may also see を marking the space you move through, though に is more standard for the destination.

Passive に入はいられる: someone entering your space uninvited. 泥棒どろぼうに入はいられた (a burglar broke in — classic suffering passive).

Causative を／に入はいらせる: making or letting someone enter. 子供こどもにお風呂ふろに入はいらせる (make the kid take a bath).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 入れる (いれる)

Negativeはいらないに

Polite negはいりませんに
Pastはいったに
Polite pastはいりましたに
て-formはいってに
Volitionalはいろうに

Causativeはいらせるを／に

Potentialはいれるに

Cond ～たらはいったらに

The angular writing system. Where hiragana is round and flowing — designed for cursive writing with a brush — katakana is sharp, geometric, built from straight lines and hard corners. Both derive from Chinese characters, but from different parts: hiragana simplified the entire character into a flowing form, while katakana took fragments of characters and kept them angular. カ comes from the left side of 加か. タ from part of 多た. Pieces broken off and repurposed.

Modern katakana has four main jobs: foreign loanwords (コーヒー, テーブル), onomatopoeia (ドキドキ, ガタガタ), emphasis (like italics in English), and scientific/technical terms (ウイルス, virus). If you see a word in katakana and don't recognize it, try saying it out loud in an English accent — there's a decent chance it's a borrowed word you already know.

The word itself: 片仮名かたかな in kanji — 片かた (fragment) + 仮名かな (borrowed characters). "Fragmentary borrowed characters." Compared to 平仮名ひらがな — 平ひら (smooth, plain) + 仮名かな. "Smooth borrowed characters." The names describe how each script looks and feels.

Japanese time words are assembled from interchangeable parts, and once you see the system, dozens of vocabulary items collapse into a simple grid. The columns are time units: 週しゅう (week), 月つき (month), 年ねん (year). The rows are position: 先せん/去きょ (last), 今こん (this), 来らい (next). Snap them together: 先週せんしゅう (last week), 今週こんしゅう (this week), 来週らいしゅう (next week). Same pattern across the grid.

The catch is readings. 今週こんしゅう (こんしゅう) uses Chinese-derived readings throughout — clean and predictable. So does 今月こんげつ (こんげつ). But then 今日 is きょう and 今年ことし is ことし — native Japanese words that were already in use for "today" and "this year" before kanji arrived, so they kept their original sounds. The regular ones (今週こんしゅう, 今月こんげつ) are gifts. The irregular ones (今日, 今年ことし) are just memorization.

今週中に終わらせます (I'll finish it within this week). That 中ちゅう suffix — "within" — attaches to any time word to set a deadline. 今日中, 今月中, 今年中. Useful in work emails.

meaning: to open (abstractly opens and spreads itself, e.g., flower, events, doors)
lesson:
Japanese has two common verbs written 開ひらく — read あく (intransitive, "it opens") and ひらく (both transitive and intransitive, with a broader, more abstract feel). This entry is ひらく, and its range is wide.

ひらく is opening in the expansive sense. A flower opens (花はなが開ひらく). A store opens for business (店みせを開ひらく). You hold an event (パーティーを開ひらく). You open a book (本ほんを開ひらく). The common thread: something that was closed, folded, or dormant unfurls and becomes accessible. It's grander and more deliberate than あく/あける, which are about physically opening doors and lids.

The kanji 開かい is 門もん (gate) with 开かい (a pair of hands) inside — hands pushing open a gate from within. The image is of something being released, spread wide, made available.

Particles: When transitive: を — 会議を開く (hold a meeting). When intransitive: が — 花はなが開ひらく (a flower blooms). Context determines which.

Passive に開かれる: に marks the organizer. 毎年この場所で祭りが開かれる (a festival is held here every year).

Causative に開かせる: making someone hold/open something.

五段 Godan自動詞／他動詞
Dictionaryひらくを／が
Negativeひらかないを／が
Politeひらきますを／が
Polite negひらきませんを／が
Pastひらいたを／が
Polite pastひらきましたを／が
て-formひらいてを／が
Volitionalひらこうを／が
Polite volひらきましょうを／が
Passiveひらかれるに
Causativeひらかせるに／を
Caus-passひらかせられるに
Potentialひらけるが
Imperativeひらけを／が
Imp なさいひらきなさいを／が
Cond ～ばひらけばを／が
Cond ～たらひらいたらを／が

The kanji 水みず in its oldest form was a literal picture of flowing water — a central current with splashes on either side. The modern character still holds that flow if you squint: the vertical stroke is the main stream, the outer strokes are tributaries splitting off.

みず specifically means cold water. Not warm, not hot. If you order 水みず at a restaurant in Japan, you get cold water — and it's free, which surprises visitors from countries where you pay for water or have to ask for it. Japanese restaurants bring お水みず (with the polite お) or お茶ちゃ automatically. The culture of free water is part of Japanese hospitality.

In Chinese-derived readings, 水みず becomes すい: 水曜日すいようび (Wednesday — water-day), 水泳すいえい (swimming), 水道すいどう (water supply/plumbing). Hot water gets its own word entirely: お湯ゆ. This split — cold water is 水みず, hot water is 湯ゆ — doesn't exist in English, and it means every reference to water in Japanese already tells you the temperature.

The third member of the demonstrative family. これ (near me), それ (near you), あれ — far from both of us. The mountain on the horizon, the car across the parking lot, the restaurant you went to last month. Physical or temporal distance, as long as it's removed from both speaker and listener.

あれ is also used for shared knowledge — things both people know about but that don't need to be named. あれ、どうなった？ (what happened with that thing?). The vagueness is the point. あれ lets you reference something without spelling it out, which is useful when the topic is sensitive, when you're being deliberately vague, or when both parties know exactly what you mean and naming it would be redundant.

Don't confuse this with the interjection あれ？ — said with a rising tone, it means "huh?" or "wait, what?" Same sound, totally different function. The interjection expresses mild surprise or confusion: あれ？鍵かぎがない (huh? my keys are gone).

Two horizontal lines. That's it. 一いち is one line, 二に is two lines, 三さん is three lines. The first three numbers in kanji are as intuitive as counting gets — and then 四し breaks the pattern completely, because stacking four horizontal lines was deemed too confusing. But for 二に, what you see is what you get.

The reading に is the Chinese-derived one, used in almost all counting and compounds: 二人ふたり (two people — irregular reading), 二月にがつ (February), 二十にじゅう (twenty). The native Japanese reading is ふた, surviving in 二ふたつ (two things) and 二人ふたり.

Watch out: 二に looks and sounds exactly like the particle に. Context keeps them apart, but it's worth noting because in certain sentences the overlap can create momentary ambiguity. 二ふたつに分わける (divide into two) — the first に is the number, the second is the particle.

Two different しめる verbs you need to keep straight. 閉しめる = to close (a door, a lid). 締しめる = to tighten, fasten, tie. The difference is in the action: closing puts a barrier in place, tightening pulls something snug.

ネクタイを締しめる (tie a necktie). ベルトを締しめる (fasten a belt). ねじを締しめる (tighten a screw). The kanji 締てい has the thread radical 糸いと — something being pulled taut, bound together. It's a physical sensation: the tug of a knot, the squeeze of a fastened strap.

The expression 気を引き締きをひきしめる (literally "tighten your spirit") means to brace yourself, to get serious, to tighten up mentally. Coaches say it before games. Bosses say it before deadlines. The metaphor is physical — slack becomes taut — applied to your mental state.

Particles: 締しめる takes を — the thing you tighten is your direct object.

Potential が締められる: が.

Passive に締められる: に marks who tightened it. 首を締められる (to be strangled — have one's neck tightened).

Causative に…を締めさせる: に marks who you make fasten, を marks what.

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 締まる (しまる)
Dictionaryしめるを
Negativeしめないを
Politeしめますを
Polite negしめませんを
Pastしめたを
Polite pastしめましたを
て-formしめてを
Volitionalしめようを
Polite volしめましょうを

Causativeしめさせるに／を
Caus-passしめさせられるに
Potentialしめられるが
Imperativeしめろを
Imp なさいしめなさいを
Cond ～ばしめればを
Cond ～たらしめたらを

The opposite of 美味おいしい. Food that tastes bad is まずい. But like many Japanese words, the meaning extends beyond the literal. A bad situation is also まずい. まずい、遅刻ちこくする！ (shoot, I'm going to be late!). それはまずいんじゃない？ (isn't that kind of a problem?). The word bridges taste and trouble — if life tastes bad, something has gone wrong.

Usually written in hiragana. The kanji 不味まずい exists (不ふ = not, 味あじ = taste — "not-tasty") but you'll rarely see it outside dictionaries. The hiragana form is so standard that using kanji would look oddly formal for such a casual word.

Be careful using まずい about someone's cooking. Like in any language, telling someone their food is bad is a social minefield. Japanese often softens it: ちょっと口くちに合あわない (it doesn't quite suit my taste) is the diplomatic option. まずい is for when you're with close friends and honesty is welcome.

Dictionaryまずい
Negativeまずくない
Pastまずかった
Past negまずくなかった
Adverbまずく
て-formまずくて

平ひら (smooth, plain, ordinary) + 仮名かな (borrowed characters). The "smooth borrowed characters" — smooth because hiragana's flowing curves were the everyday, accessible script, contrasted with the angular, abbreviated katakana. Hiragana was originally called 女手おんなで ("woman's hand") because women in the Heian court — barred from the Chinese classical education that men received — developed this cursive simplification of kanji to write Japanese. Some of the greatest works of Japanese literature, including The Tale of Genji, were written in hiragana by women.

Each hiragana character is a simplified version of a Chinese character, chosen for its sound: あ from 安あん, い from 以い, う from 宇う. The simplification was so extreme that the connection is invisible unless someone shows you the evolution. What was once calligraphic shorthand became its own complete writing system.

Today, hiragana handles grammatical particles, verb endings, native Japanese words without standard kanji, and furigana (reading guides above kanji). It's the first script children learn and the backbone that holds written Japanese together.

The kanji 曇どん tells you exactly what's happening: 日にち (sun) buried under 雲くも (cloud). The sun is still there, but something heavy sits on top of it. That's cloudiness in a single character.

くもる shares its root with 雲くも (cloud) — literally "to cloud over." But it reaches beyond weather. A mirror fogs up: 鏡かがみが曇くもる. Glasses steam up when you walk into a warm room: 眼鏡めがねが曇くもる. A person's expression darkens: 顔かおが曇くもる (their face clouds over). In every case, something that was clear becomes obscured.

The noun form 曇くもり is what you'll hear in weather forecasts: 明日あしたは曇くもり (tomorrow will be cloudy). You already learned 晴はれる (to clear up) — 曇くもる is its opposite number. Weather in Japanese often comes down to these two verbs pushing back and forth.

Particles: 曇くもる is intransitive — things cloud over on their own. The sky, a mirror, a face: they take が as the subject. 空が曇る (the sky clouds over). No direct object — nothing is being acted upon.

Passive に曇られる: indirect passive — the clouding affects you. Rare but possible: せっかくの日曜日に曇られた (it went and got cloudy on my precious Sunday). に marks the source of the frustration.

Causative を／に曇らせる: making something cloud over. を for forcing it: 息で鏡を曇らせる (fog up the mirror with your breath). に is less common here.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryくもるが
Negativeくもらないが
Politeくもりますが
Polite negくもりませんが
Pastくもったが
Polite pastくもりましたが
て-formくもってが
Volitionalくもろうが
Polite volくもりましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばくもればが
Cond ～たらくもったらが

Here's the trap: 触さわる is intransitive. In English, "touch" takes a direct object — you touch something. In Japanese, you touch to something. The thing you touch takes に, not を: 壁かべに触さわる (touch the wall). Your hand goes toward the wall and makes contact. The wall isn't being acted on — you're arriving at it.

There's a transitive counterpart: 触ふれる. It's more literary, more delicate — the lightest brush of contact, or figurative touching (touching on a topic, touching someone's heart). さわる is physical and everyday. A child reaching for something hot: 触さわらないで！ (don't touch!). That's さわる's home territory.

Museum signs in Japan almost always say 手てを触ふれないでください (using 触ふれる, polite and formal) rather than 触さわらないで. But a parent in a store says 触さわらないの！. Same meaning, different register — and different verbs.

Particles: 触さわる takes に — the thing you touch is the destination of your hand, not a direct object. 顔に触る (touch one's face). Think of it as "make contact with."

Passive に触られる: someone touches you and you don't like it. に marks the toucher. 知らない人に触られた (a stranger touched me).

Causative を／に触らせる: letting or making someone touch. に for permissive — 子供に触らせる (let the kid touch it). を for coercive.

The kanji 駄だ means "pack horse" — a horse used for carrying loads, not riding. 目め means "move" in the context of board games (a game turn or position). A wasted move on the game board — that's 駄目だめ. Originally a Go term: a point on the board that belongs to neither player, worthless territory. From there it drifted into everyday life as the universal Japanese word for "no good."

だめ does enormous work. A plan that won't succeed: だめだ. A kid reaching for cookies before dinner: だめ！ Something broken beyond repair: もうだめだ (it's done for / I can't take anymore). A person who's hopeless at something: 私わたしは料理りょうりがだめ (I'm no good at cooking). It compresses "not allowed," "won't work," "useless," and "hopeless" into two syllables.

だめもと — short for だめでもともと ("even if it fails, that's where we started") — is a common attitude phrase meaning "nothing to lose." You'll hear it when someone tries something with low expectations: だめもとで聞きいてみよう (let's ask — worst case, they say no).

Dictionaryだめだ
Negativeだめじゃない
Pastだめだった
Past negだめじゃなかった
Adverbだめに
て-formだめで

飲のむ (to drink) + 物もの (thing) = a thing you drink. One of the cleanest compound patterns in Japanese: verb stem + 物もの. You already know 食べ物たべもの (food — a thing you eat). Same blueprint.

Once you see the pattern, you can decode — and sometimes even build — new words. 買い物かいもの (shopping — things you buy). 忘れ物わすれもの (lost item — a thing someone forgot). 落し物おとしもの (dropped item). The verb stem names the action, 物もの makes it a thing. That's it.

Pronunciation note: the み in 飲のみ物もの is the verb's 連用形 (conjunctive stem). For godan verbs, this is always the い-row: 飲のむ → 飲み, 買う → 買い. This stem form is the connector that lets verbs snap onto other words.

木もく is wood, trees — but here it's Jupiter. The Japanese days of the week follow the same planetary scheme as English (which got it from Norse gods, who got it from Roman planets): Sunday=Sun, Monday=Moon, Tuesday=Mars, Wednesday=Mercury, Thursday=Jupiter, Friday=Venus, Saturday=Saturn. Japanese just uses the five Chinese elements instead of gods: 火か(fire/Mars), 水すい(water/Mercury), 木もく(wood/Jupiter), 金きん(metal/Venus), 土ど(earth/Saturn).

So Thursday in English is Thor's day (Thor = Jupiter in the Norse-Roman mapping). Thursday in Japanese is Jupiter's day. Same planet, different mythology. 木星もくせい is literally Jupiter — the "wood star." Knowing this system means you don't need to memorize seven random words. The pattern carries all of them.

This is the chassis that all seven days snap onto. 日にち = day. 曜よう = the celestial body that governs that day. Plug in the element and you've got your weekday: 月曜日げつようび, 火曜日かようび, 水曜日すいようび, 木曜日もくようび, 金曜日きんようび, 土曜日どようび, 日曜日にちようび.

The kanji 曜よう is rare outside this word. It has 日にち on the left (sun/day) and a complex right side derived from characters meaning "bright, shining." Originally it referred to the luminaries — sun, moon, and the five visible planets — the seven celestial bodies that each rule a day. The whole week-naming system came to Japan from China, which got it from Indian astronomy, which got it from Babylonian astrology. 曜日ようび carries four thousand years of stargazing in two kanji.

何曜日？ (what day of the week?) — you'll ask and answer this constantly. In casual speech, the 日にち often drops: 月曜に会おう (let's meet Monday).

Not the noodles. (Well, also the noodles — 蕎麦そば — but that's a different word.) This そば means the space right next to something. It's closer than 近ちかく (nearby). If 近ちかく is "in the area," そば is "right there, within arm's reach."

駅えきのそば (right by the station). そばにいて (stay by my side). The second example is emotionally loaded — it's the kind of thing you say to someone you don't want to leave. そば implies physical proximity with a warmth that 近ちかく doesn't carry. A description of where a building is uses 近ちかく. A request for someone to stay close uses そば.

Written sometimes as 側そば (side), but usually in hiragana. The kanji is the same one in 右側みぎがわ (right side) and 左側ひだりがわ (left side) — always about being on a particular side of something.

The casual version of こちら. Same meaning — "this direction, over here, my side" — but stripped down for everyday speech. The こ-そ-あ-ど system has a full casual set: こっち (here), そっち (there, your side), あっち (over there), どっち (which way?).

こっちに来きて (come over here). こっちのほうがいい (this one's better). こっちは準備じゅんびできてるよ (we're ready on this end). Notice how it smoothly shifts from spatial (come here) to comparative (this option) to social (our side). Like all こ-words, it marks your territory — whatever is near you or belongs to your sphere.

Don't use こっち in polite situations. Job interview? こちら. Texting a friend? こっち. The double っ gives it a punchy, informal feel — you can almost hear the thumb-jerk gesture that goes with it.

火か is fire — and Mars. Tuesday in English is Tyr's day (the Norse god of war, mapped onto Mars). Tuesday in Japanese is Mars's day. 火星かせい is Mars, the "fire star." The red planet got "fire" in Chinese astronomy because of its color, and the association stuck for four millennia.

An easy mnemonic: Mars is red, fire is red, 火か is fire. Ka-youbi — ka like "car" on fire. Tuesday burns.

If you already know 木曜日もくようび, you have the pattern: element + 曜日ようび. Now it's just a matter of memorizing which element goes with which day. The weekday elements in order (Mon-Sat) are: 月げつ火か水すい木もく金きん土ど. That sequence — moon, fire, water, wood, metal, earth — follows the traditional East Asian ordering of celestial bodies by their apparent speed across the night sky.

meaning: to become thirsty, be thirsty, dry up
lesson:
Japanese has two verbs pronounced かわく, and mixing them up is a rite of passage. 渇かわく (with the water radical + a "tongue out" component) is thirst — your body drying out from the inside. 乾かわく (with the shield radical) is drying — laundry on a line, paint on a wall, things losing moisture from the outside.

The kanji 渇かつ has 氵さんずい (water) on the left — hinting at the water you need — and 曷かつ on the right, which originally meant "to exhaust" or "why?" (as in, why is there no water?). The character captures a craving for water that isn't there.

The most common phrase: 喉のどが渇かわく (のどがかわく — to be thirsty, literally "throat dries out"). Japanese doesn't say "I'm thirsty" — it says "my throat has dried." The body part is the subject, not you. Same pattern as お腹が空く (to be hungry — "belly empties").

Particles: 渇かわく is intransitive — the throat is the subject with が. 喉のどが渇かわいた (I'm thirsty). No one is causing the thirst — it just happens to you.

Passive に渇かれる: extremely rare. Thirst doesn't really get passivized in practice.

Causative を渇かせる: making someone thirsty. 塩辛い食べ物が喉を渇かせる (salty food makes you thirsty).

五段 Godan自動詞

Negativeかわかないが
Politeかわきますが
Polite negかわきませんが
Pastかわいたが
Polite pastかわきましたが
て-formかわいてが
Volitionalかわこうが
Polite volかわきましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばかわけばが
Cond ～たらかわいたらが

Three horizontal lines. That's it. One line is 一いち, two lines is 二に, three lines is 三さん. These three are the only kanji that work this way — pure tally marks. 四し (four) abandons the system because four lines stacked up would just look like a smudge.

San is the on'yomi (Chinese reading), used in compounds and counting. The native Japanese reading み or みっ shows up in 三みっつ (three things) and 三日みっか (the third day). Three is culturally significant in Japan: 三種さんしゅの神器じんぎ (the three imperial regalia), 三回さんかい as the magic number for repetition. The folk saying 三度目の正直 ("third time's the truth" — third time's the charm).

水すい is water — and Mercury. Wednesday in English is Woden's day (Woden = Odin = Mercury in the Norse-Roman mapping). Wednesday in Japanese is Mercury's day. 水星すいせい is Mercury, the "water star."

Why water for Mercury? In Chinese five-element theory, Mercury — the fastest planet, flowing rapidly across the sky — was associated with water. Water flows, Mercury flows. The association is about movement, not chemistry.

Mnemonic: Sui-youbi. Sui sounds like "sweet" — imagine pouring sweet water in the middle of the week. Wednesday sits at the center of the workweek, the water break.

The native Japanese counting system — ひとつ, ふたつ, みっつ... — is the oldest layer of Japanese numbers, predating any Chinese influence. These are the numbers Japanese had before kanji arrived. You use them to count objects when you don't know (or don't bother with) the specific counter: りんごをふたつください (two apples, please).

ふた is the root for "two" in native Japanese. You see it everywhere once you look: 二人ふたり (two people), 二日ふつか (the second day), 二ふたつ (two things). The つ in ふたつ is the generic thing-counter — it only works up to ten (とお), after which you switch to the Chinese system (じゅういち, etc.).

今いま (now, this) + 晩ばん (evening). You already know this pattern: 今年 (this year), 今月 (this month), 今週 (this week). 今 snaps onto any time period to make it "this one."

The famous greeting 今晩こんばんは (good evening) is literally "as for this evening..." — an unfinished sentence fossilized into a greeting, just like 今日こんにちは is "as for today..." The は is the topic particle, not part of the word, which is why it's written は and not わ. The sentence that originally followed — something like "how are you?" or "how is everything?" — got dropped centuries ago, but the opening fragment survived as a standalone greeting.

The kanji looks like a person (人) wearing a hat, or a cross with a swoosh. Ancient Chinese didn't need to tally-mark their way to a thousand — they picked a character and assigned the value. Unlike 一二三, there's no visual logic here. Just memorize the shape.

Pronunciation matters with 千 because of sound changes in compounds. 千 alone is せん. But: 三千さんぜん (not さんせん). 八千はっせん (not はちせん). These shifts — called rendaku and gemination — happen because certain sounds colliding with せん make the tongue stumble, so the language smoothed them out over centuries. 一千いっせん and 八千はっせん get a double consonant; 三千 voices the せ into ぜ.

Useful benchmark: 千円 is about $7 USD (fluctuates, but close enough). A 千円札せんえんさつ is the most commonly used bill in daily life.

meaning: six days, sixth of the month
lesson:
Muika — not ろくにち. The first ten days of the month use native Japanese readings that you simply have to memorize. They don't follow a clean pattern: ついたち, ふつか, みっか, よっか, いつか, むいか, なのか, ようか, ここのか, とおか. After ten, the system switches to the Chinese readings (じゅういちにち, etc.) and life gets easier.

The む in むいか comes from 六むっつ. Over centuries of being said quickly in date contexts, むっつ + か (day) compressed into むいか. The same sound-erosion happened to all ten: the native number root fused with か and got worn smooth by a thousand years of daily use.

Three layers of politeness wrapped around one word. 姉あね is "my older sister" — humble, used when talking about your own family to outsiders. お姉さん is "(someone's) older sister" or a polite way to address your own. The お and さん bracket it in respect.

But お姉さん has escaped the family. It's what you call any youngish woman you don't know — a shop clerk, a stranger on the street, anyone who looks roughly 20-40. お姉さん、これいくらですか (Miss, how much is this?). It's casual but not rude. For older women, it shifts to おばさん — and that transition is socially fraught. Calling someone おばさん who considers herself お姉さん territory is a minor insult.

The kanji 姉 is 女おんな (woman) + 市いち (city/market). The original idea was a woman who goes to market — i.e., old enough to handle adult tasks. The elder sister.

直る is the intransitive side: the thing fixes itself, or gets fixed by unknown hands. Its transitive partner 直なおす is when you fix it. パソコンが直った (the computer got fixed) vs. パソコンを直した (I fixed the computer). Same outcome, different focus — did the thing heal, or did someone heal it?

Watch out: there's also 治なおる — same pronunciation, different kanji, different meaning. 直る fixes objects and situations. 治る heals bodies and illnesses. 時計が直った (the clock got fixed) vs. 風邪が治った (the cold got better). The kanji keeps them apart in writing, but in speech they're identical.

The kanji 直 originally depicted an eye (目) looking straight ahead, with a vertical line for emphasis. Straight, direct, correct — that's the core meaning. To 直る is to return to the correct state.

Particles: 直る is intransitive — the thing that gets fixed takes が. 車が直った (the car got fixed). Nothing is being done to it by the subject — it's reporting a result.

Passive: Not commonly used — the verb is already intransitive and describes a state change.

Causative を直らせる: rare. You'd normally use the transitive 直す instead of causativizing this.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 直す (なおす)
Dictionaryなおるが
Negativeなおらないが
Politeなおりますが
Polite negなおりませんが
Pastなおったが
Polite pastなおりましたが
て-formなおってが
Volitionalなおろうが
Polite volなおりましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばなおればが
Cond ～たらなおったらが

ちょっと is Japan's most versatile soft-no. On the surface it means "a little" — ちょっと待って (wait a sec), ちょっと高い (a bit expensive). Straightforward. But its real power is as a polite refusal machine.

今日はちょっと… (Today is a little...) — that trailing-off ちょっと with no ending is one of the most common ways to decline an invitation in Japanese. The sentence is deliberately incomplete. "It's a bit..." A bit what? Difficult? Inconvenient? You don't say. And the other person doesn't ask. Everyone understands. The vagueness is the politeness.

This is different from 少すこし, which also means "a little" but doesn't carry the social function. 少し is a precise quantity word. ちょっと is a social tool that happens to also express small amounts. You can't decline an invitation with 少し… — it doesn't work. The magic is specific to ちょっと.

Four has two readings and Japanese people actively avoid one of them. し (Chinese reading) sounds exactly like 死し (death). So in most everyday contexts, you'll hear よん instead. Hospital floors skip room 4. Gift sets avoid groups of four. The number itself isn't unlucky — its sound is.

When do you use し? Mostly in fixed compounds where the Chinese reading is locked in: 四月しがつ (April), 四季しき (four seasons), 四十しじゅう (forty). When counting freely — 四つ, 四人, 四時 — よん/よっ/よ dominates. Context decides, but when in doubt, よん is safer.

これ (this) + から (from) = from this point forward. Transparently literal — the kind of Japanese compound that rewards you for knowing the pieces.

It has two distinct flavors. The first is temporal: これからどうする？ (what are we doing now? / what's next?). The second is forward-looking and aspirational: これから頑張ります (I'll do my best from now on). In this second sense it's a common phrase at the beginning of relationships, new jobs, school years — any fresh start. これからよろしくお願いします (I look forward to working with you from here on out).

Compare with 今いまから, which means "starting right now" with more immediacy. これから is about the future in general; 今から is about what happens in the next few minutes.

You met 思う early on — gut-level thinking, opinion, impression. 考える is the other kind: deliberate, effortful reasoning. You sit down and work through something. よく考えて (think it over carefully) means "don't react — reason it out." 考えさせてください (let me think about it) is the polite stalling tactic for difficult decisions.

The kanji 考 has 老ろう (old) at the top — originally depicting an elderly person bent over a cane. The idea: the wisdom that comes from age and long reflection. Thinking as the slow accumulation of understanding, not a flash of insight. That distinction between 考える (reasoning) and 思う (feeling/believing) is one of the most important verb pairs to internalize.

Particles: 考える takes を for the topic of thought — 将来のことを考える (think about the future). It also takes と for the content of a conclusion — いいと考える (consider it good), though this use is more formal than 思う.

Potential が考えられる: が — the thing that can be considered becomes the subject. いくつかの可能性が考えられる (several possibilities can be considered).

Passive に考えられる: に marks who's doing the thinking. Often used impersonally: 原因はストレスだと考えられている (the cause is thought to be stress).

Causative に考えさせる: に marks who you make think. 生徒に自分で考えさせる (make students think for themselves).

一段 Ichidan他動詞
Dictionaryかんがえるを
Negativeかんがえないを
Politeかんがえますを
Polite negかんがえませんを
Pastかんがえたを
Polite pastかんがえましたを
て-formかんがえてを
Volitionalかんがえようを
Polite volかんがえましょうを
Passiveかんがえられるに
Causativeかんがえさせるに
Caus-passかんがえさせられるに
Potentialかんがえられるが
Imperativeかんがえろを
Imp なさいかんがえなさいを
Cond ～ばかんがえればを
Cond ～たらかんがえたらを

戻る means to go back to where you started — your point of origin, your default state, your home position. It's different from 帰る, which specifically means returning home. 戻る is more general: you can 戻る to a room you just left, to a previous topic in conversation, to your desk after lunch, to a save point in a game.

席に戻る (return to one's seat). 元に戻る (go back to the original state). 話を戻すと (getting back to the topic...) — that last one uses the transitive partner 戻す (to put something back), but it shows how flexible the concept is.

The kanji 戻 has 戸と (door) with a component inside suggesting reversal. Going back through the door you came from. The physical image anchors the abstract: return, revert, undo.

Particles: 戻る takes に for the destination you're returning to — 家に戻る (return home), 元の場所に戻る (go back to the original place).

Passive に戻られる: rare, but possible as indirect passive — someone's return affecting you.

Causative を／に戻らせる: making someone go back. 生徒を教室に戻らせる (make the students return to the classroom).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 戻す (もどす)

Polite negもどりませんに
Pastもどったに

Yet another verb read かえる. Japanese has a cluster of them: 帰る (return home), 変える (change something), 換える (exchange), 替える (replace). They're all different words with different kanji that happen to share a sound. Context and kanji keep them apart.

変える is transitive — you change something. Its intransitive pair is 変かわる — something changes on its own. 予定を変える (change the plan) vs. 予定が変わった (the plan changed). You'll need both.

The kanji 変 is also a な-adjective meaning "strange, weird": 変な人 (a weird person). The connection is intuitive — something that changes from the normal is strange. 変 carries both "to alter" and "to be altered from what's expected."

Particles: 変える takes を — the thing you change is the direct object. 色を変える (change the color). 考えを変える (change one's mind).

Potential が変えられる: が — the thing that can be changed becomes the subject. この設定が変えられる (this setting can be changed).

Passive に変えられる: に marks who changed it. 勝手にルールを変えられた (the rules were changed on me without asking).

Causative に変えさせる: に marks who you make change something. 彼に態度を変えさせた (I made him change his attitude).

て-formかえてを

Imp なさいかえなさいを
Cond ～ばかえればを
Cond ～たらかえたらを

The kanji 朝 is built from components meaning "sun" and "tide" — the sun rising with the morning tide. In ancient coastal communities, the two events were practically synonymous. The same kanji is read ちょう in compounds and means "dynasty" or "court" (the imperial audience happened at dawn).

朝 generates a whole family of time words: 朝ご飯あさごはん (breakfast), 今朝けさ (this morning — irregular reading), 毎朝まいあさ (every morning), 朝食ちょうしょく (breakfast — formal). The irregular けさ is worth flagging: it's one of those readings you just have to know. No rule predicts it.

The kanji 歯 is a picture of teeth. The top part (止) represents the upper jaw, and the bottom part shows teeth lined up in a row. It's one of the more visually grounded body-part kanji — once someone points it out, you can see the mouth with teeth in it.

You've already learned 歯ブラシ (toothbrush) — now you know where the 歯 in it comes from. Other common compounds: 歯医者はいしゃ (dentist), 歯磨はみがき (tooth brushing / toothpaste), 虫歯むしば (cavity — literally "bug tooth"). That last one is memorable: a cavity is caused by a bug eating your tooth.

Pronunciation warning: は as "tooth" sounds identical to the topic particle は and the counter 歯 (for number of teeth). Context always clarifies, but it's another reminder that Japanese relies heavily on situation to disambiguate.

meaning: to do one's best, work hard, persist
lesson:
頑張る might be the most culturally loaded verb in Japanese. It's encouragement, self-motivation, and social expectation compressed into four syllables. 頑張って！ is what you say to someone before a test, a job interview, a game, a surgery, a divorce hearing — literally any situation where someone is about to face difficulty. There is no clean English equivalent. "Good luck" is too passive. "Do your best" is too flat. 頑張って means "push through, endure, give it everything."

The kanji 頑 means "stubborn" (頑固, がんこ), and 張 means "to stretch, extend." Stubbornly stretching yourself — refusing to give up. The word captures the Japanese value of effort as virtue, regardless of outcome. You don't have to succeed. You have to try hard.

頑張ります is the polite-form pledge you'll make constantly — at work, in class, to anyone who's helping you. It signals not just intent but humility and earnestness. Learning to deploy it naturally is itself a social skill.

Particles: 頑張る is intransitive — you persevere, but you don't persevere something. Use で to mark what you're persevering at: 仕事で頑張る (work hard at the job). Use を only with specific challenges: 試験を頑張る (give the test your all).

Passive: essentially unused — persevering isn't something done to someone.

Causative を頑張らせる: making someone try hard. 子供を頑張らせすぎる (push the kid too hard).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryがんばるで
Negativeがんばらないで
Politeがんばりますで
Polite negがんばりませんで
Pastがんばったで
Polite pastがんばりましたで
て-formがんばってで
Volitionalがんばろうで
Polite volがんばりましょうで
Passiveがんばられるに
Causativeがんばらせるを／に
Caus-passがんばらせられるに
Potentialがんばれるで
Imperativeがんばれで
Imp なさいがんばりなさいで
Cond ～ばがんばればで
Cond ～たらがんばったらで

Four kanji, seven syllables, one phone. 携帯けいたい (portable/carrying) + 電話でんわ (telephone). A phone you carry. Nobody says the full thing anymore — it's always shortened to 携帯けいたい or, increasingly, just スマホ (smartphone).

The word is a time capsule from the late 1990s when mobile phones were genuinely novel and needed a descriptive name. Before smartphones, Japan's flip phones (ガラケー, short for ガラパゴス携帯 — "Galapagos phone," named because Japanese phone features evolved in isolation from the rest of the world) had their own unique ecosystem: emoji were invented for these phones, mobile internet browsing started here, and novel-length fiction written on tiny keypads became a real literary genre.

If you see 携帯 on a sign, it usually means "phones" in general — 携帯電源オフ (turn your phone off).

The kanji is a drawing of rain: a horizontal line for the sky, a box for a cloud, and four dots falling down. Oracle bone script versions are unmistakable — literal rain from heaven. It's one of the most visually transparent kanji still in use.

雨 is a radical that appears in dozens of weather-related kanji: 雪 (snow), 雲 (cloud), 雷 (thunder), 霧 (fog), 霜 (frost). Every time you see that flat-cloud-with-drops shape sitting on top of another component, you know the word is about atmospheric phenomena.

Japan has a rich vocabulary for rain types that reflects how much rain shapes daily life: 梅雨つゆ (the rainy season — literally "plum rain," because it falls when plums ripen), 小雨こさめ (light rain), 大雨おおあめ (heavy rain), にわか雨 (a sudden shower). The rainy season from June to July dominates summer planning the way winter snow dominates northern climates.

金 lives three lives. As かね it means money — blunt, casual, the way you'd talk about cash with friends. As きん it means gold — the metal, the color, the element. And as the weekday element, it's Venus/metal (金曜日, Friday).

The kanji itself depicts a smelting furnace with molten metal — two dots of ore above, a container below, and the 土 (earth) from which metals are extracted. Gold was the first metal to get its own character because it doesn't corrode, so it represented the ideal of permanence.

お金かね is the polite version — the お softens what would otherwise be a blunt topic. In conversation, you'd usually say お金がない rather than 金がない (both mean "I have no money," but the second sounds rougher, more frustrated). お金持かねもち (rich person — literally "money-holder") is a common compound. Without お, 金持ち also works but sounds more casual.
